clean_text: Alhamdulillah. We had mentioned before regarding repentance. The statement of at the beginning of this chapter that the conditions for repentance are three conditions. He mentioned that a person is contrite and has regret. That a person immediately ceases the act that they are repenting from. And number three, that the person has the sincere intention never to return to that sin that they are repenting from. We also mentioned that other scholars number the conditions of repentance as five those three and they add two conditions of intention that a person's intention is for the sake of Allah that they're not repenting from that sin for any other reason. And number two, that repentance take place that they repent during a time when repentance is accepted. We then mentioned that repentance is accepted any time in a person's life until one of two things happens. Either their soul is being taken out of their body or the sun rises from the west. The question that we have today, why is it that we find this occurs? When we read the books of the scholarship of Islam, we see one will mention three conditions for instance and another will mention five. Why is that? Why does that happen? Is it does that mean that the Quran and Sunnah are not clear? Does that mean that the Quran and Sunnah are unintelligible? Does that mean that there is a difference in the Quran and Sunnah? Does that mean that there is a difference in understanding them? None of these things are the reason. When scholars differ on things like this, most of the time, not always, but most of the time it is a categorization issue. What does that mean, a categorization issue? For instance, if a scholar says one of the conditions of repentance is that a person have the intention of repenting for the sake of Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala. Any act of worship that is done without total sincerity to Allah is considered void. We know this. This is the meaning in practice ofahill. What is the meaning ofahillah in practice? That every act of worship be done sincerely and entirely for the sake of Allah. When some scholars mention that as a as a condition on its own and others in Noah do not mention it. Do they really differ on that being a condition of repentance? No, they do not differ on that being a prerequisite. However, and Noah would say, well, technically, technically speaking, a person cannot be truly regretful and truly have the intention never to do it in the future unless they were doing it for the sake of Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala. So really those conditions include the condition of intentions. And what about the issue of repenting during the time when repentance is accepted? Some scholars say this is a fifth and final condition that if you want to repent, a fifth condition of repentance is that your repentance be in a time when repentance is accepted. and Neoi and those scholars that are of the view that it is three conditions they would rebuttal and say well technically this is not a condition these two times would be considered what is meaning when is repentance accepted all the time it is easier to mention and it is more reasonable to mention the times when repentance is not accepted than it is to mention the time when repentance is accepted. That means that a person's time is mostly spent during a time when repentance is accepted. Therefore, when repentance is not accepted, this is not a condition of repentance. This is a manner of repentance. Meaning naturally, the natural course of things, the majority of a person's life in this world, repentance is accepted until one of these two events occurs. Being that these are events that occur, that means that these are things that bar a person's repentance rather than being a time when repentance is accepted. So we see that these two opinions, they're really what they're saying the same thing. They're just saying them in a different way, using different terms. And that goes back to what the precision of knowledge and words and categorization. And that precision is desperately needed. Why? Because other rulings of Islam are going to be built based on how that categorization is put in place and structured. However, at the final practical level, when it comes to you or I repenting, all of those things have to be included. Whether we were to say the conditions of repentance are three or the conditions of repentance are five. Sometimes scholars will choose a more a more comprehensive opinion when teaching especially the general masses of people. Why? Because it's more comprehensive. for instance, the conditions ofahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahillah. For those who haven't heard of this before many of us might actually be a little bit surprised when we say the conditions ofahillah every act of worship in Islam has conditions, pillars and obligations. Every act of worship salah has conditions, it has pillars, has obligations. Fasting has conditions, pillars, obligation, zaki, etc. Any act of worship has conditions, pillars and obligations. what it is structured by and what are necessary ingredients for it to be accepted by Allah is the greatest act of worship. It is what we were created for. And all of this that we're doing salah zak is all just what is all just an embodiment of the implications ofahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahillah it's just sayingahillahill in different ways saying it with our body saying it with our wealth saying it with what we do not put into our body saying it what with what we do with our body all of it is essentially saying And soahill of course has conditions. It has pillars. It has obligations. The conditions ofahillah are seven. Seven conditions. Some scholars mention an eighth condition. What's the difference? There is no difference. They all agree on the ingredients. They just did they just differ on what categorization to put the ingredients in. And so those who say seven that is more scholarly when we look at how it is structured. It is more precise when when we look at the opinion of those who say that the conditions ofahill are eight conditions. that is more that is more appropriate in teaching the general masses of people. Why? Because it is more it is more specific for them. It outlines things that people normally wouldn't think of on their own. So repentance has three conditions and some scholars say it as five. But as we'll hear in the next narration that they all agree on the ingredients, they just differ on how these ingredients are categorized. So in Noah tells us that the conditions of repentance are three. Number one, a person is ceases the act that they are repenting from the sin. They stop doing it. Number two, they are contrite. They have regret. Number three, they have the they have the true intention with earnesty that they will not return to doing that sin. Their intention is never to go back to doing that sin. He then mentions the narration of who says that the messenger of Allah said he then mentions the narration of collected by Muslim in which the messenger of Allah sallall.aii alaihi wasallam said whoever repents before the sun rises from its west from its setting point then Allah will accept his repentance we see that this is one of the ingredients the other scholars mention this it's just that neoi does not consider this a condition rather he cons gives it a different categorization but they agree on all the necessary components in order for repentance to be acceptable. Subhanahu wa tala. Subhuhammed. Alhamdulillah.

summary: This lecture discusses the conditions of repentance (tawbah) in Islam, explaining why Islamic scholars differ in their categorization of these conditions. The speaker clarifies that while some scholars mention three conditions for repentance (ceasing the sin, feeling regret, and having sincere intention never to repeat it) and others mention five conditions (adding intention for Allah's sake and repenting during an accepted time), these differences are primarily matters of categorization rather than substantive disagreement. The lecture emphasizes that all scholars agree on the essential ingredients necessary for repentance to be accepted by Allah; they simply organize these ingredients differently based on scholarly precision and teaching methodology. The speaker uses this discussion to illustrate a broader principle in Islamic jurisprudence: that differences in scholarly opinions often stem from how concepts are organized and categorized, which has important implications for building subsequent rulings.
suggested_title: Understanding Scholarly Differences in the Conditions of Repentance (Tawbah)
hadith_chapter: Narrations on repentance and acceptance before sunrise from the west
